---
bibliography: refs.bib
---

## Research Process Flowchart
The research process for this study was structured to ensure a systematic and rigorous approach to data collection, analysis, and interpretation. The flowchart below illustrates the sequential steps taken in the study, from the initial planning and design of the survey to the final analysis and interpretation of the data. Each step was carefully planned and executed to ensure that the research objectives were met and that the findings were robust and reliable (@fig-rp-flowchart).

![Flowchart of the research process, from the initial planning and design of the survey to the final analysis and interpretation of the data. The flowchart illustrates the sequential steps taken in the study, including survey design, data collection, data analysis, and reporting of results.](/misc/research_process_flowchart.png){height=70% #fig-rp-flowchart}

## Distance Sampling
Distance sampling is a widely established and rigorously validated methodology for the systematic estimation of animal abundance and distribution [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015; @bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001]. The method is based on the principle that the probability of detecting an animal decreases with increasing distance from the observer, and by modelling this detection probability, unbiased estimates of population density can be derived [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015; @bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010]. Since its formalisation, Distance sampling has been successfully adapted across a broad range of terrestrial, aerial, and aquatic survey contexts, demonstrating considerable methodological flexibility [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015].

Within the field of marine mammal science, Distance sampling has become one of the most frequently applied methods for estimating cetacean abundance and distribution [@dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @raudinoAerialAbundanceEstimates2023], forming the basis of large-scale systematic surveys conducted by organisations including the `#gls("iwc")`{=typst} and the `#gls("noaa")`{=typst} [@AbundanceCetaceansCalifornia; @macleodAbundanceAtlanticWhitesided2004; @stephanisSummerSpatialDistribution2008].Shipboard line transect surveys, in particular, have been employed extensively to monitor cetacean populations across a range of geographic scales, from oceanic basins to coastal and riverine habitats [@AbundanceCetaceansCalifornia; @lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @dickUsingDistanceSampling2011; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @thomasDistanceSoftwareDesign2010].

## Survey design
This study addresses that gap by conducting the first dedicated systematic cetacean survey within `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}, applying Distance Sampling methodology [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001; @thomasDesigningLineTransect2023]. This area poses some unique challenges that need to be addressed while designing the survey. To meet the basic assumptions for a good survey design mentioned by Thomas et al. [-@thomasDesigningLineTransect2023] the starting point of the survey was chosen randomly using a random seed for every transect design within the `dssd` package [@marshallDssdDistanceSampling2025]. To minimise the time traveling between survey legs (off-effort time) an equal spaced zigzag design was chosen. The zigzag design was layed out so there are only a minimal number of apexes, to avoid biases caused by responsive movement of the species [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008].

To avoid the survey legs to spread, due to the shape of the survey area [@strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonSmallboatSurveysCoastal2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008] the area was split into three strata (East, South and West, see @fig-area). Within those strata the zigzag design followed the longest axis of the area and as perpendicular to the coastline as possible [@strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008; @thomasDesigningLineTransect2023; @strindbergZigzagSurveyDesigns2004; @dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008]. The survey vessel followed this designed survey as closely as possible, while maintaining a speed of 10 knots to ensure even coverage and effort [@dawsonDesignFieldMethods2008]. A Garmin 78s GPS Device was used to check the current location of the vessel as well as record the track and locations of the sightings.

![Sampling area divided in the three strata (East, South and West) in order to achieve effetive transects. Zigzag transects are overlayed (orange) and follow the longest axis of the strata. The starting point of the transects will be shifted every survey.](/figures/region_transects.png){#fig-area}

In order to generate reproducible and standardised results, the observers followed the survey guidelines (see @app-protocol) for the survey protocol and data collection methods (for checklist @app-checklist). The survey was conducted in accordance with the ethical guidelines for marine mammal research, ensuring minimal disturbance to the animals and their habitats.

By comparing systematic outputs against existing opportunistic datasets (OBIS, https://mapper.obis.org/), this study will also critically evaluate the relative value of each monitoring approach for informing conservation and management decisions in geographically bounded marine territories.


## Photo Identification (Photo ID)
Additionally to the data collected through the systematic distance sampling, opportunistic photo identification data was collected, to further assess species composition and distribution within the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}. Small cetaceans, such as short-beaked common dolphin (*Delphinus delphis*) and striped dolphins (*Stenella coeruleoalba*), along with bottlenose dolphins (*Tursiops truncatus*) are the most common encountered species in the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst} [@canales-caceresDistributionBehaviourStriped2023; @gnoneCetaceansMediterraneanSea2023; @olaya-ponzonePhotoIdentificationCatalogueDelphinus2025; @olaya-ponzoneMixedspeciesDolphinGroups2025; @olaya-ponzoneSpatioTemporalPatternsLimited2026; @olaya-ponzoneVesselInducedBehavioural2025]. 
Because of the expected species in this region, the method to capture photo id will be modified and applied to small cetaceans [@wuersigMethodsPhotoidentificationSmall1990; @roselPhotoidentificationCapturemarkrecaptureTechniques2011]. This data will be used as supplemental material to further understand the species distribution inside the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}.

## Land-based Survey
Land-based cetacean surveys represent a viable and cost-effective alternative to the more expensive vessel-based surveys. The utilisation of vantage points facilitates a more profound interpretation of the observed behaviour. In the context of behavioural studies, land-based surveys have been a particularly effective method of data collection, as they do not influence the behaviour of cetaceans or introduce bias via the survey vessel [@mannBehavioralSamplingMethods1999].
A **series** of surveys was conducted, with observers remaining on-effort for a period of 10 minutes, followed by a 20-minute break. This protocol was implemented to ensure the observers could rest and the cetaceans could leave the area, reducing the likelihood of double-counting [@gutierrez-munozPatternsTrendsCetacean2021]. Three locations were selected to cover the majority of the `#gls("bgtw")`{=typst}: Europa, Prince George and Sandy Bay (@tbl-landspots, @fig-spots).


| Station | Latitude | Longitude | Height (m a.s.l.) | Scan sector
|---------|------|------|------|------|
| Europa | 36°06′33.48″ N | 5°20′44.34″ W | 18 | 060–220°
| SandyBay | 36°07′47.99″ N | 5°20′31.65″ W | 82 | 016–155°
| PrinceGeorge |36° 6' 50.4" N | 5° 20' 58.5096"  | 82 | 207–335°

: Land-based observation stations, with position, height above sea level and the compass sector scanned from each. {#tbl-landspots}

The areas were sampled **12** times in total split up in morning and afternoons. Surveys were postponed whenever the visibility was too low or the sea state exceeded five. 

![Point of views of the three selected vantage points (Prince George (A), Sandy Bay (B) and Europa Point (C), facing left (a), right (b) and a wide shot of the spot (c). Overview of the area covered by each spots sector (D).](/figures/spots_comb.png){#fig-spots}

Observations were carried out both with the naked eye and using binoculars (Fujinon 7 × 50) using the continuous horizon “scanning” method [@mannBehavioralSamplingMethods1999]. When an observation was made, the time, number of reticles, horizontal bearing, species and group size were documented; the presence of birds or fish was also noted, where possible. When possible a camera (Canon EOS R50) with a telephoto lens (Canon 100 – 400 mm) was used to gather additional photos in order to confirm the species and behaviour later.

## Data Analysis
### Calculate distance {#calc-dis .unnumbered}
To calculate the distance of the sighting ($d$), the number of reticles ($r$) from the horizon line or, in the case of the western side, the coastline, was recorded. Utilising the observers' height ($h$), the reticle factor ($F$) and the provided formula, the distance can be calculated [@bucklandIntroductionDistanceSampling2001]. Implementing the earths radius ($R$) was used to make the formula more accurate [@lerczakCalculatingSightingDistances1998]:

$$
d=\frac{h}{\tan{\left(r\times F+\sqrt{\frac{2\times h}{R}}\right)}}
$$

Using the distance and the bearing, the location can be calculated and plotted.

Because of the enclosed nature of the western survey area (the Bay of Algeciras, Gibraltar), the far shore of the bay rather than the sea horizon was visible along most bearings from the Westside station. As the reticle method derives distance from the angular separation between a sighting and a known reference line, the standard horizon-based formula could not be applied here: the coastline sits closer than the true horizon and therefore at a different (greater) angle of depression, so treating it as the horizon would overestimate sighting distances.

For this station the reference depression angle was derived from the distance to the coastline along each sighting's bearing instead. Using a detailed polygon of the bay's landmasses (including the detached mole off Algeciras, which forms the first visible obstacle on some bearings), a ray was projected from the observation point along the recorded horizontal bearing, and the distance to the first intersection with land was taken as the reference distance ($d_{ref}$). The depression angle of the reference line was then calculated as:

$$
\theta_{ref}=\frac{h}{d_{ref}}+\frac{d_{ref}}{2R}
$$

where $h$ is the observer height and $R$ the Earth's radius, with the second term correcting for the curvature of the sea surface between observer and coastline. This replaces the horizon dip term ($\sqrt{\frac{2\times h}{R}}$) used at the open-water stations; the two expressions are equivalent when $d_{ref}$ equals the horizon distance. Bearings that pointed out of the bay mouth, where no coastline was intersected, reverted to the standard horizon reference. The sighting distance was then obtained from the total depression angle ($\theta_{ref}+reticleAngle$) as before (see @app-disR or [GitHub](https://github.com/datsimon-design/Masters_Project) for example in R).

## Statistical Analysis
All statistical analyses were carried out in R [@zotero-item-2907]. `#gls("spue")`{=typst}, calculated as the number of sightings per on-effort scan, was strongly right-skewed and zero-inflated and did not meet the assumptions of parametric analysis of variance (normality of residuals and homogeneity of variance); a non-parametric approach was therefore adopted, with each on-effort scan treated as the sampling unit. Differences in SPUE among the three active observation stations (Europa, SandyBay and PrinceGeorge) were tested using a Kruskal–Wallis rank-sum test [@KruskalWallis1952]. Where the omnibus test was significant, pairwise differences were identified using Dunn's post-hoc test [@dunnMultipleComparisonsUsing1964], which operates on the pooled ranks of the Kruskal–Wallis test and accounts appropriately for tied values; p-values were adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini–Hochberg false discovery rate procedure [@benjaminiControllingFalseDiscovery1995].

## Detection Function {#det-func .unnumbered}
As the Distance package requires a flatfile [@bucklandDistanceSamplingMethods2015] the data from the effort log and the sighting log were first mutated and then joined, the effort was calculated by dividing the sector degrees by 360. The area for each wedge was calculated and added to the data frame. The `ds` function was then used to fit models with different key functions to the data, specifically half-normal and hazard-rate. The best fitting model was select according to the lowest `#gls("aic")`{=typst} and goodness of fit, using the `gof_ds` and `AIC` function and a Q-Q plot. After deciding on which key function fitted best, the keyfunction in addition with covariates were tested to see if they improved the model fit. The covariates tested were height and spot, as well as both together. 

Because the area was seperated into three strata (@fig-spots) the best fitting model was passed to the `dht2` function with the strata as strata formula. This function was then used to calculate the relative abundance of dolphins per spot.

## Distribution Map
`#gls("kde")`{=typst} was used to describe the relative spatial distribution of cetaceans within the BGTW. The locations of all on-effort sightings, derived from reticle-based radial distances and compass bearings (see [Calculation](#calc-dis)). A bivariate kernel density surface was then computed in R using the `ks` package [@duongKsKernelSmoothing2005], with each sighting weighted by its group size so that the surface reflected the relative density of individuals rather than of sightings alone. The smoothing bandwidth was selected automatically using a plug-in bandwidth matrix [@duongPluginBandwidthMatrices2003], and the density was evaluated over a 100 × 100 grid spanning the survey area. Because the resulting Kernel density estimation has been a useful tool to assess spatial distribution and relative abundance of cetaceans in research the surface is a normalised probability density (integrating to one), it provides a descriptive, relative measure of spatial concentration and was not corrected for observation effort or imperfect detection; these were instead accounted for separately through the distance-sampling density estimation ([Detection Function](#det-func)). The kernel density surface was visualised over the study area, and overlaid with the observation stations and individual sightings, to identify areas of elevated cetacean occurrence.

As there has been difficulties getting the university’s boat in the water the boat surveys could not be conducted. Thanks to a private charter and a one time trip with the dolphin safari RHIB, two boat-surveys could be conducted. Those two trips only covered parts of the area as the first one was planned as pilot study and the dolphin safari RHIB was not able to follow the designated transect line. The sightings recorded from the vessel-based surveys were as such excluded from the analysis.

This study was carried out under the University of Gibraltar approval, Ethics ID: 259/2026/UniGib
